label: Components
heroTitle: Understand the building blocks you will mention in almost every architecture discussion.
heroDescription: These are the parts engineers combine to make systems faster, safer, easier to scale, and easier to operate.
cardIntro: Open a component to see what it does, when to use it, simple examples, and common interview trade-offs.

items.title: Load Balancer
items.summary: A load balancer spreads traffic across multiple servers so one server does not take everything.
quickPoints: It improves reliability and better uses server capacity.
It can stop sending traffic to unhealthy servers.
It is often the front door of a service.
items.whatItMeans: A load balancer sits in front of a group of servers and decides which one should receive each request.
items.whyItMatters: Without it, one server can become overloaded while others sit idle. It also becomes harder to survive server failures.
items.simpleExample: Think of a receptionist sending customers to the next free counter instead of making everyone stand in one line.
realWorldExamples: An ecommerce site sends checkout traffic across many app servers.
A media service routes users to healthy streaming gateways.
An internal API platform uses a load balancer to spread traffic across containers.
items.interviewTip: Mention health checks, routing policy, and whether sessions must stay on the same server.
tradeOffs: Sticky sessions make some apps easier, but hurt even traffic distribution.
Global routing improves user latency, but adds more networking complexity.
One load balancer is simple, but you need redundancy to avoid a single point of failure.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that the load balancer itself must also be reliable.
Ignoring session state when requests can land on different servers.
Using round robin only because it sounds simple, even when smarter routing is needed.

sections.title: What it usually does
paragraphs: A load balancer receives traffic first, checks which backend servers are healthy, and forwards the request to one of them.
bullets: Can terminate TLS and enforce simple policies.

items.title: Cache
items.summary: A cache stores often-used data in a faster place so users do not wait on slower systems each time.
quickPoints: Caches reduce repeated work.
They improve response time and reduce database pressure.
They can return stale data if updates are not handled carefully.
items.whatItMeans: A cache is a temporary storage layer that keeps data likely to be needed again soon.
items.whyItMatters: Repeatedly reading the same item from a database is wasteful. A cache can make hot data much faster to serve.
items.simpleExample: Keeping your most used notebooks on your desk is faster than walking to a storage room every time.
realWorldExamples: A product page cache stores popular item details.
A CDN caches images near users.
A social app caches hot profiles and trending posts.
items.interviewTip: Explain where the cache lives and how stale data is handled.
tradeOffs: Fast reads come with invalidation complexity.
Big caches reduce backend load, but increase memory cost.
Long TTL values improve hit rate, but can show old data longer.
commonMistakes: Saying 'add cache' without describing what data is cached.
Ignoring cache misses and stampedes.
Treating cached data as if it is always fresh.

sections.title: Common cache locations
paragraphs: Caching can happen in the browser, CDN, app server, or database side. Each location solves a different performance problem.
bullets: Browser cache: good for static assets.
CDN cache: good for global content delivery.
Application cache: good for hot database reads.

items.title: Queue
items.summary: A queue lets one service hand work to another service later instead of doing everything right away.
quickPoints: Queues smooth traffic spikes.
They decouple producers from consumers.
They are useful when work can happen after the user gets a response.
items.whatItMeans: A queue stores tasks or messages until another service is ready to process them.
items.whyItMatters: Not every job must complete during the user request. Queues help systems stay responsive and more stable under bursty traffic.
items.simpleExample: A bakery can take orders quickly and place them in a line for the kitchen instead of baking every cake at the front desk.
realWorldExamples: A signup service queues welcome emails.
A video platform queues transcoding jobs after upload.
A marketplace queues search indexing after product updates.
items.interviewTip: Call out retry behavior, duplicate messages, and what happens if consumers fall behind.
tradeOffs: Queues improve resilience, but add delay.
At-least-once delivery is robust, but consumers must handle duplicates.
Ordering can be useful, but strict ordering may reduce scale.
commonMistakes: Using a queue even when the user needs an immediate answer.
Ignoring dead-letter queues for repeated failures.
Assuming messages are processed exactly once without extra work.

sections.title: When to use a queue
paragraphs: Use a queue when you want a request to stay fast, but there is follow-up work that can happen in the background.

items.title: CDN
items.summary: A CDN stores content close to users so they do not always fetch it from the main origin server.
quickPoints: CDNs reduce latency for global users.
They lower load on the origin.
They work best for static or cacheable content.
items.whatItMeans: A CDN is a network of edge servers placed in many locations. Users fetch content from a nearby edge instead of a far-away origin whenever possible.
items.whyItMatters: Distance matters. Users far from the origin server see slower responses, especially for large media files.
items.simpleExample: Instead of shipping every package from one far city, you store popular items in local warehouses near customers.
realWorldExamples: A video app serves thumbnails and segments from a CDN.
A learning site serves stylesheets and images from edge nodes.
A gaming platform distributes patches worldwide through edge caches.
items.interviewTip: Mention what is cacheable, how updates are invalidated, and whether dynamic content still goes to origin.
tradeOffs: CDNs reduce latency, but invalidating stale content can take time.
Edge delivery is fast, but personalized content may still need origin logic.
Global reach helps user experience, but increases configuration complexity.
commonMistakes: Sending dynamic personalized responses to a CDN without clear cache rules.
Ignoring cache invalidation for changed content.
Assuming CDN use solves all performance problems.

sections.title: Best fit
paragraphs: CDNs are best for content many users request, especially static files and media. They are less useful for every response that is unique per user.

items.title: Database Replica
items.summary: A database replica is an extra copy of data used for failover, reads, or both.
quickPoints: Replicas can improve read scale.
They help availability during failures.
Replication lag can make reads stale.
items.whatItMeans: A primary database accepts writes, and one or more replicas keep copies of the same data.
items.whyItMatters: A single database is often a bottleneck and a risk. Replicas give more read capacity and help recovery when the primary fails.
items.simpleExample: If one notebook has the official answers and others have copies, people can read from copies while one person updates the main notebook.
realWorldExamples: An ecommerce catalog uses replicas for product page reads.
A ride platform keeps replicas for read-heavy dashboards.
A reporting system reads from replicas so it does not slow down live writes.
items.interviewTip: Explain where writes go, where reads go, and whether stale reads are acceptable.
tradeOffs: More replicas increase read capacity, but add replication cost.
Replica lag can hurt correctness for fresh reads.
Failover improves resilience, but promotion logic must be safe.
commonMistakes: Assuming replicas instantly match the primary.
Sending important read-after-write traffic to a lagging replica.
Treating replication as a replacement for backups.

sections.title: How to describe read scaling
paragraphs: A common pattern is write to the primary and read from replicas for queries that do not need the newest value immediately.

items.title: API Gateway and Proxy
items.summary: An API gateway gives one front door for clients and handles shared concerns before requests reach services.
quickPoints: It can handle auth, rate limits, routing, and logging.
It simplifies clients by giving one entry point.
It should not become a giant bottleneck.
items.whatItMeans: An API gateway or proxy sits between clients and backend services. It can route requests, enforce rules, and hide internal service details.
items.whyItMatters: As systems grow, you do not want every client to know every backend service directly.
items.simpleExample: A hotel front desk handles requests and directs guests to the right department instead of asking each guest to find the kitchen, housekeeping, and billing team alone.
realWorldExamples: A mobile app sends all API requests through one gateway.
A company uses a gateway for authentication and rate limiting.
A multi-service platform hides internal service URLs from clients.
items.interviewTip: Good answers mention what belongs in the gateway and what should stay in the business services.
tradeOffs: One gateway simplifies clients, but can become a central bottleneck.
Shared policy logic is useful, but too much business logic at the edge becomes messy.
Centralized auth is convenient, but gateway failures affect many services.
commonMistakes: Putting too much product logic into the gateway.
Ignoring rate limiting and request validation at the edge.
Treating the gateway as if it can replace service boundaries.

sections.title: What usually lives here
paragraphs: A gateway is a good place for authentication, routing, request validation, basic rate limiting, and common logging.

items.title: Databases in More Detail
items.summary: Databases are not all the same. Different products need different storage shapes, access patterns, and guarantees.
quickPoints: Choose the database based on reads, writes, and data rules.
SQL and NoSQL are tools, not team identities.
Indexes, joins, and write patterns matter as much as database labels.
items.whatItMeans: This topic covers how to choose between relational, key-value, document, wide-column, or graph style storage based on the work the system does most often.
items.whyItMatters: Many interview answers become weak when the database is chosen by habit instead of by access pattern.
items.simpleExample: A payments table with strong relationships often fits SQL well, while a simple session store often fits key-value storage better.
realWorldExamples: Orders and payments often use relational databases.
Session storage often uses key-value systems.
Content metadata may use document stores when the schema changes often.
items.interviewTip: Say what your main reads and writes are first. Then explain why one database model fits them better.
tradeOffs: SQL gives stronger relational structure, but horizontal scaling can be harder.
NoSQL can scale very well, but joins and strict consistency may be weaker.
Using many storage systems improves fit, but increases operational complexity.
commonMistakes: Saying NoSQL is always better for scale.
Ignoring indexes and query patterns.
Choosing one database for every kind of data.
seniorConsiderations: The best storage design often uses more than one storage type, but only when the complexity is justified.
Data ownership between services should stay clear so schemas do not become tangled.
Production storage choices should consider migration and operational maturity, not just ideal theory.
multiRegionNotes: Relational and document stores behave very differently once you add cross-region replication.
Some systems keep write ownership regional to reduce cross-region conflict cost.
observabilityNotes: Track slow queries, index hit rate, replica lag, and error rate by query type.
Watch how schema changes affect read and write latency over time.
securityPrivacyNotes: Choose storage with retention, encryption, and audit needs in mind.
Sensitive columns may need stronger access controls than the rest of the dataset.

sections.title: A simple way to choose
paragraphs: Ask whether you need strong relationships, flexible schema, huge write scale, graph traversal, or very fast key lookups.

items.title: Search Systems
items.summary: Search systems help users find the right content quickly, even when they do not know the exact text.
quickPoints: Search is not the same as a normal database query.
Search systems often use separate indexes.
Freshness and ranking are key trade-offs.
items.whatItMeans: A search system builds indexes over text and fields so users can search by words, prefixes, filters, and ranking rules.
items.whyItMatters: Many products need search, but databases built for transactions are usually not enough for good text search.
items.simpleExample: If users search for 'red shoes size 9', the system should understand filters and relevance, not just exact string matching.
realWorldExamples: Product search in ecommerce.
Message search in chat tools.
Video and article search in media apps.
items.interviewTip: Explain the write path too. Search indexes often update asynchronously after the main database changes.
tradeOffs: Async indexing keeps writes fast, but search results may lag.
Rich ranking improves results, but adds complexity.
Full-text indexes are powerful, but need extra infrastructure.
commonMistakes: Treating search like a simple SQL LIKE query at large scale.
Ignoring indexing lag.
Forgetting filters, sorting, and typo tolerance.

failureScenarios.title: Search results are stale after product updates
failureScenarios.issue: The main database is correct, but search still shows old data.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Check the indexing pipeline, event lag, failed consumers, and reindex strategy before blaming the search engine itself.

seniorConsiderations: Search quality depends on indexing freshness, ranking strategy, and operational recovery tooling.
At scale, you need to think about reindexing and schema evolution, not just search queries.
observabilityNotes: Track indexing delay, query latency, zero-result rate, and ranking-quality signals.
Separate source-of-truth correctness issues from search-index freshness issues.

sections.title: Common architecture
paragraphs: A normal pattern is main database for source of truth and a separate search index for fast querying.

items.title: Object Storage
items.summary: Object storage is built for large blobs such as images, videos, backups, and file chunks.
quickPoints: It is good for large files, not rich relational queries.
It usually scales better than storing blobs in a database.
Metadata often lives somewhere else.
items.whatItMeans: Object storage keeps files as objects with IDs and metadata, usually inside buckets or namespaces.
items.whyItMatters: Modern systems often store media and documents outside the main database because file workloads are very different from transactional data.
items.simpleExample: A photo app stores the actual image file in object storage but keeps image owner, tags, and permissions in a metadata database.
realWorldExamples: Video originals and segments.
User uploads and backups.
File chunks for sync platforms.
items.interviewTip: Separate blob storage from metadata storage when you explain file-heavy products.
tradeOffs: Object storage scales very well, but random small updates to one object can be awkward.
It is durable, but access latency may be higher than memory or SSD caches.
Metadata queries usually need another storage layer.
commonMistakes: Putting large files directly into the main relational database without a reason.
Ignoring upload resume and chunking.
Forgetting access control and signed URLs.

sections.title: Typical split
paragraphs: A practical design stores the blob in object storage and stores object metadata, owner, and sharing rules in another service.
bullets: CDN for fast delivery when public or cacheable.

items.title: Message Broker
items.summary: A message broker is the system that stores, routes, and delivers messages between services.
quickPoints: It is more than just a queue.
It helps services communicate safely and asynchronously.
Ordering, durability, and retries matter.
items.whatItMeans: A message broker accepts messages from producers and makes them available to consumers using defined delivery rules.
items.whyItMatters: As systems grow, direct service-to-service calls can become slow, fragile, and tightly coupled.
items.simpleExample: Instead of every team member calling each other directly for updates, they leave messages in a managed system that delivers them reliably.
realWorldExamples: Order events sent to billing and shipping.
Video processing jobs sent to workers.
Analytics pipelines receiving event streams.
items.interviewTip: Mention retention, delivery guarantees, retries, and how consumers recover after failure.
tradeOffs: Brokers improve decoupling, but add operational complexity.
Durable storage is safer, but slower than in-memory only delivery.
Strict ordering is helpful, but often limits scale.
commonMistakes: Using a broker without describing the delivery model.
Ignoring poison messages and dead-letter handling.
Assuming exactly-once delivery is easy.

failureScenarios.title: Consumers fall behind during a traffic spike
failureScenarios.issue: Messages pile up faster than consumers can process them.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Discuss backpressure, consumer scaling, priority handling, and whether some work can be dropped or delayed safely.

seniorConsiderations: At scale, backpressure strategy matters as much as basic delivery.
Message schemas need versioning and clear ownership.
Broker retention choices affect replay, cost, and debugging power.
observabilityNotes: Track producer rate, consumer lag, dead-letter growth, and redelivery counts.
Alert on sustained lag before it reaches user-facing impact.

sections.title: What to cover in interviews
paragraphs: Explain what produces messages, what consumes them, how long they stay, and what happens when consumers fall behind.

items.title: Pub/Sub
items.summary: Pub/Sub lets one event reach many listeners without the sender knowing them all.
quickPoints: Publishers send events to a topic.
Subscribers receive the events they care about.
It is useful for fan-out and loose coupling.
items.whatItMeans: In publish-subscribe systems, producers publish events to a named topic and one or more subscribers receive copies.
items.whyItMatters: Many systems need one update to trigger several actions in different services.
items.simpleExample: When an order is placed, billing, inventory, notifications, and analytics may all need the event.
realWorldExamples: User signup triggers email, analytics, and profile setup.
New post events trigger feed updates and notifications.
Price update events trigger search index and cache refresh.
items.interviewTip: Differentiate pub/sub from a single-consumer queue. In pub/sub, many subscribers can react to the same event.
tradeOffs: Pub/Sub improves fan-out, but makes tracing event paths harder.
Many subscribers add flexibility, but also create debugging complexity.
Loose coupling is good, but schema versioning becomes important.
commonMistakes: Calling every async system pub/sub when it is really point-to-point queueing.
Ignoring duplicate or late events.
Not planning for event schema changes.
seniorConsiderations: Pub/Sub systems need clear event contracts and consumer ownership rules.
Fan-out can amplify bad events very quickly, so validation and replay tooling matter.
Tracing events through many subscribers becomes an observability problem, not only a delivery problem.
observabilityNotes: Track per-topic publish rate, subscriber lag, and dropped or failed event processing.
Use event tracing to see where fan-out chains become slow or fail.

sections.title: Best use cases
paragraphs: Pub/Sub is strong when one event should trigger many downstream actions without tightly coupling the publisher to each service.

items.title: WebSocket
items.summary: WebSocket keeps a long-lived connection open so servers and clients can push updates quickly.
quickPoints: Useful for real-time experiences.
Avoids repeated polling for every update.
Connection scale and state management become important.
items.whatItMeans: A WebSocket connection stays open after it starts, so the server can push data to the client without waiting for a new request each time.
items.whyItMatters: Realtime apps like chat, trading, presence, or live dashboards need low-latency updates.
items.simpleExample: A chat app using WebSocket can deliver new messages instantly without the phone asking every few seconds.
realWorldExamples: Chat and group messaging.
Live sports scoreboards.
Realtime order status or ride tracking.
items.interviewTip: Explain how you handle many open connections, reconnects, and which data still needs durable storage elsewhere.
tradeOffs: Realtime updates feel fast, but long-lived connections use server resources.
WebSocket reduces polling waste, but load balancing sticky behavior may be needed.
It is good for delivery, not for long-term storage.
commonMistakes: Using WebSocket as if it replaces the database.
Ignoring reconnect and missed-message recovery.
Assuming one server can handle unlimited open connections.

failureScenarios.title: Connection storm after a network glitch
failureScenarios.issue: Large numbers of clients reconnect at the same time after a brief outage.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Use reconnect backoff, load balancing, and session recovery logic so the reconnect wave does not overload the system.

seniorConsiderations: Open-connection scale affects infrastructure shape and failure handling.
Realtime systems need a clean recovery story for missed updates.
Connection-heavy systems often need load balancing, session affinity, and strong gateway observability.
observabilityNotes: Track connection count, reconnect spikes, send latency, and dropped delivery events.
Watch for one gateway becoming hot after uneven balancing.

sections.title: What else you still need
paragraphs: Realtime delivery usually still needs storage, queues, presence tracking, and retry logic around it.

items.title: Rate Limiting
items.summary: Rate limiting protects systems by controlling how much traffic a user, client, or service can send.
quickPoints: It protects reliability and fairness.
Checks must be fast.
It often lives at the edge or gateway.
items.whatItMeans: Rate limiting tracks request counts in a time window and blocks or slows requests that go over a defined limit.
items.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting, one noisy user, attacker, or buggy client can harm everyone else.
items.simpleExample: A login page may allow only a small number of attempts per minute to reduce abuse.
realWorldExamples: Public APIs limiting free-tier usage.
Message sending limits to reduce spam.
Checkout or payment APIs protecting against overload.
items.interviewTip: Say who is limited, where the limiter runs, and whether burst traffic should be allowed.
tradeOffs: Strict limits improve protection, but may hurt legitimate users during spikes.
Distributed counters scale better, but increase coordination complexity.
More precise algorithms are fairer, but usually cost more.
commonMistakes: Not defining the identity being limited.
Using slow persistent storage for every check.
Applying the same limits to every endpoint.

goodVsWeakAnswer.question: What is a strong rate-limiting explanation?
goodVsWeakAnswer.weakAnswer: I would use Redis to rate limit the API.
goodVsWeakAnswer.goodAnswer: I would define the identity key first, choose whether bursts are allowed, place the limiter near the edge, and use a fast counter store so checks stay cheap and consistent enough for the use case.
goodVsWeakAnswer.whyGoodWorks: The stronger answer explains the design decisions around the tool instead of naming the tool only.

seniorConsiderations: Rate limiting is a product and abuse-control decision, not just an infra feature.
Different endpoints may need different identities, limits, and burst behavior.
Overload policies should work together with backpressure and priority rules.

sections.title: What to decide first
paragraphs: You need to decide the identity key, the time window, the allowed burst behavior, and the storage for counters.

items.title: Auth and Session Handling
items.summary: Authentication proves who the user is. Session handling keeps track of that user across requests.
quickPoints: Auth and session logic belong in most real products.
You need a safe way to identify users again after login.
State can be stored in signed tokens or server-side sessions.
items.whatItMeans: Authentication checks identity, while session handling remembers that identity after login so users do not need to log in on every request.
items.whyItMatters: Many interview systems have user accounts, permissions, and multi-device behavior. Weak auth design can break the whole product.
items.simpleExample: After logging in to an online store, you stay signed in while browsing products and checking out.
realWorldExamples: Web apps using cookies and server sessions.
Mobile apps using access tokens and refresh tokens.
Admin panels with stronger session controls and expiration.
items.interviewTip: Explain what kind of client you have and how sessions are refreshed, expired, revoked, and protected.
tradeOffs: Server-side sessions are easy to revoke, but require shared session storage.
Tokens scale well, but revocation and refresh rules need careful design.
Long sessions improve convenience, but increase security risk.
commonMistakes: Mixing authentication and authorization as if they are the same thing.
Ignoring session expiration and logout behavior.
Forgetting CSRF, token leakage, or refresh token misuse.

failureScenarios.title: Token leakage incident
failureScenarios.issue: A session token is exposed in logs or client storage.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Revoke affected tokens, rotate secrets if needed, audit access, and review storage and transport protections.

seniorConsiderations: Auth design affects product safety, abuse handling, and incident response.
Session strategy should match client type, revocation needs, and risk level.
Permission checks and audit needs matter as much as the login flow itself.
securityPrivacyNotes: Protect tokens in transport and storage.
Use strong expiry and revocation strategy for risky sessions.
Audit who accessed sensitive actions and when.

sections.title: Simple interview framing
paragraphs: A practical answer says how users log in, what identity token or session is used after that, where permissions are checked, and how sessions are refreshed or revoked.
